prompt:
Our job runner currently stores schedules as raw interval seconds and product wants real cron expressions ('0 9 * * mon-fri' and friends). I do NOT want croniter as a dependency for this — write us a small engine, cronex.py, that we own.

API: `parse(expr)` returns a schedule object with two methods: `matches(dt)` (does this naive datetime land on the schedule, ignoring seconds) and `next_run(after)` (the next scheduled datetime STRICTLY after `after`, whole-minute resolution — if `after` is exactly on a scheduled minute, you return the following one). Datetimes are plain stdlib datetime objects handed to you; never touch the wall clock, the scheduler owns time.

Expression dialect — classic 5 fields (minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week), each field a comma list of: `*`, a number, a range `a-b`, or either of those with a `/step` (so `*/15`, `10-40/10`). Months and weekdays also accept 3-letter names, any case (jan, MON, Sat), including in ranges like feb-mar. Day-of-week runs 0-7 where both 0 and 7 are Sunday. And the one everyone forgets, which we WILL support correctly because real crontabs rely on it: when day-of-month and day-of-week are BOTH restricted (neither is *), the day matches if EITHER matches — that's how '0 0 13 * 5' fires on Friday the 13th and every other Friday and every other 13th. When only one is restricted, it alone decides.

Garbage in = ValueError: wrong field count, out-of-range numbers, reversed ranges, step of 0, unknown names, empty list items. Ops fat-fingers crontabs weekly; I want loud failures at parse time, not silent never-fires.

next_run has to be able to jump far ahead — '0 0 29 2 *' from 2026 must find 2028-02-29 without timing out.

Acceptance contract is test_cronex.py — `python3 test_cronex.py` must pass, don't edit it. Stdlib only.
